"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>

Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

[[Category:Kumbhīra]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumbhīra}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kumbhīra]][[Category:11]] <!--Kumbhīra--> [[Category:Kumbhīra]]'''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

<!--Kumbhīra--> '''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

revision.text:
'''宮比羅'''（くびら）は、[[仏教]]の[[水神|水運の神]]で、[[天竺]][[霊鷲山]]の鬼神で、[[薬師如来]][[十二神将]]の筆頭である。'''宮毘羅'''、'''金毘羅'''、'''金比羅'''、'''禁毘羅'''とも書く。[[梵語]]では'''クンビーラ'''({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}})または'''キンビーラ'''（{{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}、「何を恐れることがあろう」の意）で、『月灯三昧経』のサンスクリット原本ではKimpiloと綴られる<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020年、46頁。</ref>。十二神将としては'''宮比羅大将'''ともいう。
== 由来 == [[ヒンドゥー教]]の[[ガンジス川]]の鰐の神クンビーラが仏教に取り入れられ宮比羅大将となった。[[薬師瑠璃光如来本願功徳経]]では、「爾時衆中有十二薬叉大将俱在会坐。所謂'''宮比羅大将'''...」と薬師如来十二神将の筆頭に挙げられ、「此十二薬叉大将。一一各有七千薬叉以為眷属。」「若有流布此経或復受持薬師琉璃光如来名号恭敬供養者。我等眷属衛護是人。皆使解脱一切苦難。諸有願求悉令満足。」と説かれる。
[[金光明経]]鬼神品第十三では、「聴是経者 摩尼跋陀 大鬼神王 富那跋陀 及'''金毘羅''' 阿羅婆帝 賓頭盧伽 黄頭大神 一一諸神 各有五百 眷属鬼神」と金毘羅の名で記される。
[[大宝積経]]菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二では、「爾時世尊。於彼中道不移其処 令諸長者建立聖果 以如来威勢入王舍城 四衆囲繞容儀庠序 時有護王舍城諸天薬叉大善神王 名'''金毘羅''' 作如是念 今者如来形相殊異 於世間中最勝難遇 堪受人天之所供養 我等今当応以種種上妙供具奉獻如来 作是念已 便以最勝飲食具足香味成就妙色 奉上於仏 爾時世尊 愍其所獻故為納受 時'''金毘羅王'''所領大薬叉衆六万八千在虚空中咸生随喜 以清遠音唱言 善哉善哉 時'''金毘羅'''即以此義告其衆曰 我已奉仏上妙供具 汝等亦応以諸供養施苾芻僧 当令汝等於長夜中利益安楽 諸薬叉衆受王教已 即以上供施苾芻僧 時諸僧衆哀受其供...」と詳細に説かれる。
宮比羅大将は[[弥勒菩薩]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
同じくヒンドゥー教の神で[[毘沙門天]]の前身である[[クベーラ]]と名前が似るためよく混同されるが両者は全く別の神格である。また、宮比羅の起源は鰐の神クンビーラではなく、仏教説話において仏陀を守ったとされる[[ヤクシャ]]のクンビーラではないかという指摘もある<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |issue = |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 |accessdate=2021-4-18 }}</ref>。
== 金毘羅信仰 == クンビーラは元来、[[ガンジス川]]に棲む[[ワニ|鰐]]を神格化した水神で、日本では[[蛇]]型とされる。クンビーラはガンジス川を司る女神[[ガンガー]]の[[ヴァーハナ]]（乗り物）でもあることから、[[讃岐国]]象頭山松尾寺金光院（現在の[[金刀比羅宮]]）では海上交通の守り神の'''金毘羅大権現'''として信仰されてきた。特に舟乗りから信仰され、一般に大きな港を見下ろす山の上で金毘羅宮、金毘羅権現社が全国各地に建てられ、[[金毘羅権現]]として祀られていた。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[金毘羅権現]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:くひら}} [[Category:天部]] [[Category:夜叉]] [[Category:護法神]] [[Category:海神]] [[Category:河川神]] [[Category:水運]] [[Category:金刀比羅神社|+くひら]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[宮比羅]] to [[Kumbhīra]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''宮比羅'''（くびら）は、[[仏教]]の[[水神|水運の神]]で、[[天竺]][[霊鷲山]]の鬼神で、[[薬師如来]][[十二神将]]の筆頭である。'''宮毘羅'''、'''金毘羅'''、'''金比羅'''、'''禁毘羅'''とも書く。[[梵語]]では'''クンビーラ'''({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}})または'''キンビーラ'''（{{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}、「何を恐れることがあろう」の意）で、『月灯三昧経』のサンスクリット原本ではKimpiloと綴られる<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020年、46頁。</ref>。十二神将としては'''宮比羅大将'''ともいう。
== 由来 == [[ヒンドゥー教]]の[[ガンジス川]]の鰐の神クンビーラが仏教に取り入れられ宮比羅大将となった。[[薬師瑠璃光如来本願功徳経]]では、「爾時衆中有十二薬叉大将俱在会坐。所謂'''宮比羅大将'''...」と薬師如来十二神将の筆頭に挙げられ、「此十二薬叉大将。一一各有七千薬叉以為眷属。」「若有流布此経或復受持薬師琉璃光如来名号恭敬供養者。我等眷属衛護是人。皆使解脱一切苦難。諸有願求悉令満足。」と説かれる。
[[金光明経]]鬼神品第十三では、「聴是経者 摩尼跋陀 大鬼神王 富那跋陀 及'''金毘羅''' 阿羅婆帝 賓頭盧伽 黄頭大神 一一諸神 各有五百 眷属鬼神」と金毘羅の名で記される。
[[大宝積経]]菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二では、「爾時世尊。於彼中道不移其処 令諸長者建立聖果 以如来威勢入王舍城 四衆囲繞容儀庠序 時有護王舍城諸天薬叉大善神王 名'''金毘羅''' 作如是念 今者如来形相殊異 於世間中最勝難遇 堪受人天之所供養 我等今当応以種種上妙供具奉獻如来 作是念已 便以最勝飲食具足香味成就妙色 奉上於仏 爾時世尊 愍其所獻故為納受 時'''金毘羅王'''所領大薬叉衆六万八千在虚空中咸生随喜 以清遠音唱言 善哉善哉 時'''金毘羅'''即以此義告其衆曰 我已奉仏上妙供具 汝等亦応以諸供養施苾芻僧 当令汝等於長夜中利益安楽 諸薬叉衆受王教已 即以上供施苾芻僧 時諸僧衆哀受其供...」と詳細に説かれる。
宮比羅大将は[[弥勒菩薩]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
同じくヒンドゥー教の神で[[毘沙門天]]の前身である[[クベーラ]]と名前が似るためよく混同されるが両者は全く別の神格である。また、宮比羅の起源は鰐の神クンビーラではなく、仏教説話において仏陀を守ったとされる[[ヤクシャ]]のクンビーラではないかという指摘もある<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |issue = |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 |accessdate=2021-4-18 }}</ref>。
== 金毘羅信仰 == クンビーラは元来、[[ガンジス川]]に棲む[[ワニ|鰐]]を神格化した水神で、日本では[[蛇]]型とされる。クンビーラはガンジス川を司る女神[[ガンガー]]の[[ヴァーハナ]]（乗り物）でもあることから、[[讃岐国]]象頭山松尾寺金光院（現在の[[金刀比羅宮]]）では海上交通の守り神の'''金毘羅大権現'''として信仰されてきた。特に舟乗りから信仰され、一般に大きな港を見下ろす山の上で金毘羅宮、金毘羅権現社が全国各地に建てられ、[[金毘羅権現]]として祀られていた。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[金毘羅権現]]
{{Buddhism2}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:くひら}} [[Category:天部]] [[Category:夜叉]] [[Category:護法神]] [[Category:海神]] [[Category:河川神]] [[Category:水運]] [[Category:金刀比羅神社|+くひら]]

'''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Konpira Shrines]]

As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

[[sn:Kumbhīra]] [[ja:宮比羅]] [[sn:umbhīra]] [[en:Kumbhīra]]
<!--Kumbhīra--><!--Kumbhīra-->

'''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.

"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>

[[sn:Kumbhīra]] [[ja:宮比羅]] [[sn:umbhīra]] [[en:Kumbhīra]]
<!--Kumbhīra--><!--Kumbhīra--> [[Category:Yakshas]] [[Category:Deva (Buddhism)]] [[Category:Water transport]] [[Category:河川神]] [[Category:Sea and river deities]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[:en:India|India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11454976]] Wikidata name: Kumbhīra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[:en:Water|Water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[:en:India|India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454976]] Wikidata name: Kumbhīra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[:en:Water|Water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[:en:India|India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

<!--Kumbhīra--><!--Kumbhīra--> [[Category:Yakshas]] [[Category:Deva (Buddhism)]] [[Category:Water transport]] [[Category:River deities]] [[Category:Sea and river deities]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11454976}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454976]] Wikidata name: Kumbhīra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[:en:Water|Water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[:en:India|India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

{{wikidata link|Q11454976}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Deva (Buddhism)]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines|+Kumbhīra]] [[Category:River deities]] [[Category:Sea and river deities]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Water transport]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Yakshas]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454976]] Wikidata name: Kumbhīra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] deity of [[:en:Water|Water]] and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of [[:en:Vulture_Peak|Vulture Peak]] in [[:en:India|India]]. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Bhaiṣajyaguru]] (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In [[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]], the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with [[:en:Kubera|Kubera]] (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with {{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|WD=Q223617|lt=Kubera}} (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumbhīra]][[ja:宮比羅]]
'''Kumbhīra''' (宮比羅, くびら) is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} deity of {{ill|Water|en|Water|qid=Q283|lt=Water|1=Water}} and [[:en:Transportation|transportation]], originally a demon deity of {{ill|Vulture_Peak|en|Vulture_Peak|qid=Q19755572|lt=Vulture Peak|1=Vulture Peak}} in {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. Kumbhīra is the leader of the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaiṣajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Medicine Buddha). It is also written as '''宮毘羅''', '''金毘羅''', '''金比羅''', or '''禁毘羅'''. In {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}, the name is '''Kumbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kumbhīra}}) or '''Kimbhīra''' ({{lang|sa|Kimbhīra}}), meaning "What is there to fear?" In the original Sanskrit text of the ''Candrapradīpa Samādhi Sūtra,'' it is spelled as ''Kimpilo''.<ref>密教図像学会『密教図像』第38号、[[法藏館]]2020th year 、46頁。</ref> As one of the Twelve Heavenly Generals, Kumbhīra is also called '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''.
== Origins == The Hindu deity Kumbhīra, a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]], was adopted into Buddhism and became Kumbhīra Daishō. The ''Bhaiṣajyaguru-vaiḍūrya-prabhā-rāja Sūtra'' describes Kumbhīra as the leader of the Twelve Yakṣa Generals:
"At that time, among the gathering, there were twelve Yakṣa Generals present. The first was '''Kumbhīra Daishō'''. ... Each of these Twelve Yakṣa Generals had 7,000 Yakṣas as their retinue. ... If anyone spreads this sūtra, upholds the name of Bhaiṣajyaguru, or makes reverential offerings, we will protect them, ensuring their release from all suffering and granting their wishes."<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
In the ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'' (Golden Light Sūtra), the name appears as "Kumbhīra," one of the protective deities of the Dharma, listed among a retinue of demons and deities.<ref>金光明経鬼神品第十三。</ref>
In the ''Mahāratnakūṭa Sūtra,'' Kumbhīra is depicted as offering food and support to the Buddha:
"Then the Lord said to Kumbhīra and his retinue, 'You have provided these offerings. Your retinue must also offer alms to the bhikṣus.' Following these instructions, the Yakṣas distributed offerings to the bhikṣus, who compassionately accepted them."<ref>大宝積経菩薩蔵会第十二之二金毘羅天受記品第二。</ref>
Kumbhīra Daishō is often linked to [[Maitreya]] as a reflection of the future Buddha.
Though often confused with {{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|qid=Q223617|lt=Kubera|1=Kubera}} (Vaiśravaṇa), the two deities represent different figures, with Kumbhīra's origins potentially linked to Buddhist yakṣa guardians.<ref>{{Cite journal|author = 水野善文 |title=故地のクンビーラ：金毘羅由来説再考 |journal = 智山学報 |volume = 65 |pages = 115 |publisher= 智山勧学会 |date = 2016 |doi=10.18963/chisangakuho.65.0_0103 }}</ref>
== Kumbhīra Worship == Kumbhīra originated as a crocodile deity of the [[:en:Ganges_River|Ganges River]] and became associated with snakes in Japan. As the vāhana of the river goddess [[:en:Ganga|Ganga]], Kumbhīra is venerated as the protector of sea and river transportation. In Japan, Kumbhīra is enshrined at [[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]], where he is worshipped as the guardian deity of sailors and travelers under the name '''Kompira Daimyōjin'''. Shrines dedicated to Kumbhīra, commonly known as Kompira Shrines, are found across Japan, often located on hills overlooking large harbors.
